children.name: README.md
children.content:
# DARSHBOT — Tic Tac Toe

A Tic Tac Toe AI that learns entirely through reinforcement learning. No hardcoded rules.

## How it works

Board positions are encoded as vectors and fed through a neural network. Q-table updates happen in real time as the AI plays. It figures out strategy on its own — I don't tell it what a good move looks like.

## Stack

- Python
- PyTorch
- Pygame

## What I learned

Reinforcement learning is harder to debug than supervised learning. When the AI does something weird, you can't easily trace why — the reward signal is the only feedback.

children.name: README.md
children.content:
# DARSHBOT — Othello

Same RL approach as the Tic Tac Toe bot, applied to a significantly harder game.

## How it works

Encodes board positions as vectors, runs through a neural network, updates game state in real time. Full reinforcement learning pipeline — evaluates positions, predicts optimal moves, adapts dynamically.

## Stack

- Python
- PyTorch
- Pygame

## What's different from the TTT bot

Othello has a much larger state space and delayed reward signals — the board can look great mid-game and still lose badly. Getting the reward shaping right took a lot of iteration.

children.name: README.md
children.content:
# AI Chatbot

A personalized chatbot that answers questions about me using stored context. Designed to embed in a portfolio site.

## How it works

Frontend in HTML/CSS/JS. Each query hits a backend that calls the Cerebras API with my context data attached. Prompt engineering handles consistent output format.

## Stack

- Cerebras API
- HTML / CSS / JavaScript

## What I focused on

Privacy safeguards — the bot has consent checks and won't surface private information. Also spent time on prompt patterns so it doesn't hallucinate things about me.

children.name: README.md
children.content:
# Comber

A fast-paced 1v1 duel game with recharging abilities: dash, lift, shoot.

## Design goal

High skill ceiling, low skill floor. Easy to pick up, hard to master. The heal station requires precise platforming to reach — so recovering mid-fight is a skill expression, not a given.

## Stack

- Unity
- C#

## What I focused on

Ability balancing — each move needed to feel powerful without breaking the game. A lot of playtesting went into cooldown timings and hitbox sizes.

children.name: README.md
children.content:
# RetroDoom

A Wolfenstein-style 3D raycaster running on a RetroPy console — RP2040 chip, 320×240 screen, 264KB RAM.

## What it has

Full raycasting engine, maze levels, enemies, guns with recoil, keys, and exits. In MicroPython. On a microcontroller.

## Stack

- MicroPython
- SPI
- RP2040

## What made this hard

Every optimization had to be aggressive: integer math instead of floats, precomputed lookup tables, minimal overdraw. Getting real-time 3D rendering in MicroPython on constrained hardware meant rethinking fundamental engine assumptions from the ground up.

children.name: README.md
children.content:
# Coin Maniac

A fast-paced browser game where you collect coins and dodge obstacles. Tracks high scores, responsive controls, dynamic difficulty.

## Stack

- HTML / CSS / JavaScript

## Notes

Built entirely with frontend web tech — no frameworks, just DOM manipulation and real-time game logic. More involved than it sounds once you get into collision detection and score persistence.

children.name: README.md
children.content:
# Maze Generator

A procedurally generated, playable maze in Scratch.

## How it works

At each step the program randomly decides to turn, places a tile, and backtracks on collision. The result is a fully connected, navigable maze every time.

## Stack

- Scratch

## Notes

This was my first real encounter with procedural content generation. The backtracking logic was surprisingly tricky to get right in Scratch's visual blocks.

children.name: README.md
children.content:
# AsciiCraft

Web app for converting images to ASCII art with real preprocessing — not just a brightness-to-character map.

## What it does

Applies contrast, brightness, gamma correction, edge detection, and dithering before mapping to characters. Multiple character sets. Smart memory optimization for large images. Exposes endpoints for image analysis, conversion, and ASCII download.

## Stack

- Python / Flask
- Pillow, NumPy, SciPy
- HTML / CSS / JavaScript

children.name: README.md
children.content:
# Turtle Master

Converts any image into functional Python Turtle code that recreates it with a controllable number of lines.

## How it works

Analyzes pixel data, resizes as needed, generates drawing instructions. You control how many lines the output uses — fewer lines means a more abstract result.

## Stack

- Python
- PIL
- Turtle

## Why

Wanted to see how far I could push Turtle — it's a basic educational library, not meant for this. Turns out you can get surprisingly detailed output if you're careful about line placement.

children.name: README.md
children.content:
# Ultimate Ciphex

Encrypts any text — including emojis and Unicode — into fully ASCII-friendly output. Fully reversible.

## How it works

Combines XOR, Base64, and two custom-designed algorithms. Practically infinite key space. The output is always safe to copy and paste anywhere.

## Stack

- Python
- CustomTkinter
- base64, unicodedata, pyperclip

## What I learned

Designing your own cipher forces you to think about what actually makes encryption hard to reverse — and most naive approaches fall apart quickly.

children.name: README.md
children.content:
# MC Stronghold Finder

Calculates the nearest Minecraft stronghold using just two Eyes of Ender throws.

## How it works

Applies the Law of Sines to triangulate coordinates from two observed angles. You note where you're standing and which direction the Eye points — twice from different positions — and the tool gives you exact coordinates.

## Stack

- Python
- Math

## Why

Finding strongholds manually is tedious. This turns it into a 30-second task.

children.name: README.md
children.content:
# Wordl-assIST

A Wordle solver powered by information theory.

## How it works

Encodes game state into vectors, evaluates entropy-based probabilities for each possible guess, and iteratively narrows the solution space. Shows best guesses and top alternatives after each round.

## Stack

- Python
- CustomTkinter
- Information Theory

## What I learned

Entropy as a decision metric is elegant — the best guess isn't the most likely word, it's the word that gives you the most information regardless of the outcome.

children.name: README.md
children.content:
# Hangman's Savior

Optimal Hangman letter-guessing using information theory.

## How it works

Evaluates letter probabilities in real time given the current known word state and updates guessing strategy dynamically. Built right after Wordl-assIST — similar math, different problem structure.

## Stack

- Python
- CustomTkinter
- Information Theory

children.name: README.md
children.content:
# GIS — Graphed Image System

An experimental image compression system that encodes images as geometric boundary data instead of raw pixels.

## Pipeline

Posterize to a compact palette → segment into uniform color regions → trace boundaries as contours → serialize to a custom `.gis` binary format compressed with LZMA.

## Stack

- Python
- Pillow

## Results

Outputs PSNR and file-size comparisons vs PNG and JPEG. Works best on flat-region, clean-boundary illustration-style images — struggles with photographs.

children.name: README.md
children.content:
# Valorant Stat Puller

Pulls detailed stats from a player's last five Valorant matches.

## How it works

Uses Python's requests library to fetch real-time data from HenrikAPI, parses the JSON responses, and formats them into structured readable output.

## Stack

- Python
- HenrikAPI

## Notes

Straightforward but useful. First time I dealt with a real third-party API with rate limits, pagination, and inconsistent field presence.

children.name: README.md
children.content:
# Leaderboard

A Pygame leaderboard built for a school science fair to track hovercraft race times.

## What it does

Players enter name and score, the program sorts and displays results in a clean UI. Uses JSON for persistent data storage.

## Stack

- Python
- Pygame
- JSON

children.name: README.md
children.content:
# Voice Calculator

A calculator that listens to spoken arithmetic and answers out loud.

## How it works

Uses Google Translate for speech-to-text input, performs the calculation, then text-to-speech for output. Built in App Inventor. Handles single-operation math questions in real time.

## Stack

- App Inventor
- Google Translate (STT/TTS)

children.name: README.md
children.content:
# Rubik's Cube Solver — Lego 51515

A robotic Rubik's Cube solver. Scans each tile, computes the solution, executes it mechanically.

## How it works

A basket-like container handles yaw rotation. A grabber arm manipulates the top layer for roll. The software scans tile colors, runs a solving algorithm, and translates moves into motor commands.

## Stack

- LEGO Mindstorms 51515
- MicroPython

## What was hard

Mapping abstract cube moves (U, R, F, etc.) to specific motor sequences for this particular robot arm geometry. A lot of physical calibration.

children.name: README.md
children.content:
# Writer — Lego 51515

A LEGO robot that writes text on paper.

## How it works

Converts input text into mechanical handwriting. Forward movement handles the X-axis; an oscillating mechanism handles Y-axis pen strokes. Each letter has preprogrammed motion sequences.

## Stack

- LEGO Mindstorms 51515
- MicroPython

## What I learned

Coordinate-based motion planning and the precision limits of LEGO motors. Letters look recognizable but not clean — the motor resolution isn't fine enough for smooth curves.

children.name: README.md
children.content:
# ESP32 Tank

A networked ESP32 tank with a procedurally generated tile floor and a detailed tank sprite.

## How it works

Key inputs (move, shoot, defensive strafe) are sent via sockets from a Python/Pygame controller to the ESP32, which updates movement in real time. Includes a fast-strafe defensive mechanic and an "inactive signal jammer" tactical feature.

## Stack

- Python / Pygame (controller)
- ESP32
- Sockets

## What this covers

Embedded systems, networked control, procedural visuals, and game feel — all in one project.

children.name: README.md
children.content:
# Portable PC Stats

Streams live CPU/GPU temperature, load, and RAM usage to a 128×64 OLED display on an ESP32-S3.

## How it works

A Python script pulls data from LibreHardwareMonitor and sends it over serial. The firmware on the ESP32 parses the stream and renders it on the display.

## Stack

- Python (host side)
- C++ / ESP32-S3 (firmware)
- SPI / Serial
- LibreHardwareMonitor

## What this covers

Every layer: host-side data extraction, serial protocol design, firmware parsing, display rendering.

children.name: README.md
children.content:
# JKLM BombBot

Automates JKLM BombParty — a multiplayer word game where you enter words containing a given syllable under a countdown.

## How it works

Bot clicks the input field, copies the current syllable via clipboard, queries a preloaded word list, and types a valid match. Prioritizes words with rare or unused letters to trigger the extra-life mechanic.

## Stack

- Python
- PyAutoGUI
- pyperclip

## What I focused on

Greedy strategy optimization — winning BombParty isn't just about answering fast, it's about draining the rare letter pool.

children.name: README.md
children.content:
# Osu!Mania Bot

A lane-reading bot for osu!mania.

## How it works

Samples fixed pixel coordinates on each of the four note lanes at high poll rate. When a lane's pixel deviates from its inactive color, the corresponding key is injected. No ML, no image recognition — pure color-threshold detection.

## Stack

- Python
- mss (screen capture)
- PyAutoGUI

## Why this approach

The game's fixed lane geometry and consistent note rendering make color detection reliable and very low latency. ML would be overkill and slower.

children.name: README.md
children.content:
# Prank Batch & VBS

A toolkit of VBS and Batch scripts automating keyboard input and system behaviors.

## What's in it

Simulated keypress sequences, recursive command flows, controlled screen-lock triggers. Uses Windows Script Host, SendKeys-style input emulation, and process recursion.

## Stack

- Batch
- VBS

## Why it's interesting

A low-level dive into OS automation and input injection — understanding how Windows scripting actually works at the system level.

children.name: README.md
children.content:
# Minecraft Server Hosting

Hosted a Minecraft server for a class — ~5 players average, up to 20.

## How

Used Playit.gg's free tunneling to safely expose the server without port forwarding. Automated start/stop with batch scripts. Handled debugging, player management, and network troubleshooting as server admin.

## Stack

- Networking
- Playit.gg tunneling
- Batch scripts

## What I learned

Server administration is mostly debugging things that shouldn't be broken. DNS propagation, port conflicts, player connection issues — all real problems with real fixes.

children.name: README.md
children.content:
# 67speed Score Spoofer

A proof-of-concept exploit against 67speed.com, a hand-motion speed game with a public leaderboard.

## How it works

Intercepts outgoing score submissions via mitmproxy, rewrites the payload to 1000 (just below the server-side flagging ceiling). No client modification — purely network-layer.

## Stack

- Python
- mitmproxy
- JSON

## What this demonstrates

Why leaderboard integrity requires server-authoritative scoring. If the client submits its own score and the server trusts it, the leaderboard is meaningless.

children.name: README.md
children.content:
# PolyTrack Time Spoofer

Intercepts and rewrites time-submission payloads in PolyTrack (a Trackmania-style browser racing game) to near-zero values.

## What happened

Spoofed times held first place on public leaderboards for ~15 hours before anticheat flagged them — revealing that integrity checks are deferred, not real-time.

## Stack

- Python
- mitmproxy
- JSON

## What this reveals

The gap between real-time leaderboard trust and asynchronous server-side validation. The leaderboard showed false data for 15 hours. Most players would never know.

children.name: README.md
children.content:
# Deadaimbot.io

A computer vision aimbot for Deadshot.io (browser-based FPS).

## How it works

Captures the screen, uses OpenCV to detect enemy health bars in real time, then repositions the cursor slightly below each bar to align with the head hitbox — exploiting the consistent UI-to-model offset.

## Stack

- Python
- OpenCV
- ctypes

## What makes it interesting

No memory reading, no game client modification. Purely vision-based from screen pixels. Shows that aimbots don't need deep access — a camera pointed at a screen would work the same way.

children.name: README.md
children.content:
# Portfolio

You're looking at it.

## Concept

Instead of a normal scrolling portfolio, the site is a simulated desktop environment. Visitors open app windows to discover content rather than scrolling through sections.

## Stack

- Vanilla HTML, CSS, JavaScript
- No frameworks, no build step
- Font Awesome
- JetBrains Mono

## What I learned

Building a fake OS UI from scratch — window management, drag interactions, macOS-style dock magnification, file explorer navigation. More involved than a normal portfolio but more interesting to build and to use.

children.name: README.md
children.content:
# Domain Acquisition

Purchased and configured a personal domain end-to-end through Cloudflare.

## What I set up

DNS records (A, CNAME, MX), debugged propagation delays and cached negative lookups, configured SMTP for transactional email.

## Stack

- Cloudflare Registrar
- Cloudflare Dashboard

## What I learned

DNS is mostly waiting and reading documentation carefully. TTL management, propagation timings, and the difference between what a record says and what resolvers have cached are all real gotchas.

children.name: about.md
children.content:
# About

I'm Darsh. 13 years old. I build things.

## What I spend time on

Python mostly — tools, automation, AI experiments. Unity when I feel like making a game. Hardware projects when I want something physical. Web stuff when the project calls for it.

## How I got here

Started with Khan Academy's JavaScript course. Weird beginning, but sure. After which, I devolved into Scratch. Moved to Python when Scratch stopped being interesting. Kept going because the skill ceiling and fun factor were both really high. Recently, I then started doing some Unity and C# alongside electronics and embedded systems.

## What I'm figuring out

How to finish projects instead of just starting them. Systems that stay reliable over time. Code that doesn't embarrass me a month later.

## What I'm not

A startup founder. (for now)  Not passionate about disrupting anything. Not trying to sound impressive. Just building things.

children.name: contact.md
children.content:
# Contact

**Email:** darsh@singhals.org

If you want to talk about something I've built, a collaboration, or anything interesting — email is the move.

I don't respond to generic outreach.
